entries.title: What is ReDoS
entries.body:
Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS) is an attack that exploits the fact that some regular expressions can take an extremely long time to process. This causes the application or service to stop responding or crash.

The essence: with an "evil" pattern (evil regex), a specially crafted string forces the engine to try a huge number of matching variants (catastrophic backtracking) before it realizes there is no match.

entries.title: Where to look for ReDoS
entries.body:
• Any user input that is later validated by a regular expression (validation of email, phone numbers, URLs, names, passwords).
• Places where the regex itself is taken from user input or configuration (search, filters, rules).
• Server-side and client-side code: both backend validation and JS form validation are vulnerable.
• First look in the pattern for "dangerous" constructs: nested quantifiers and overlapping alternations (see Evil Regex), then craft a string for backtracking.

entries.title: What an evil regex consists of
entries.body:
An Evil Regex contains:

• Grouping with repetition.
• Inside the repeated group:
  - Repetition.
  - Alternation with overlapping.

meta.note: 20 'a' characters followed by '!'. On such input the engine tries all possible ways to group the 'a' characters before realizing there is no match because of the '!' - this is what causes the explosion in the number of backtracking attempts. Suitable for patterns like (a+)+ and ([a-zA-Z]+)*.

entries.title: What backtracking and the limit are
entries.body:
Backtracking in regular expressions occurs when the engine tries to match a pattern, hits a mismatch, and goes back to the previous matching position to try another path. This process can repeat a great many times - especially on complex patterns and large input strings.

Sometimes you can force a regex to exceed more than 100,000 recursions, which causes ReDoS and leads to preg_match returning false.

entries.title: Exceeding the backtracking limit in PHP (preg_match -> false)

meta.note: When the recursion limit is exceeded, preg_match returns false (not "no match", but a runtime error). If the code treats false as "validation failed / no match", this can be used for a bypass or DoS.

entries.title: Tools for finding ReDoS
entries.body:
tjenkinson/redos-detector - https://github.com/tjenkinson/redos-detector
A CLI and library that checks with certainty whether a pattern is safe from ReDoS. Works in the browser, Node, and Deno.

doyensec/regexploit - https://github.com/doyensec/regexploit
Finds regular expressions vulnerable to ReDoS.

devina.io/redos-checker - https://devina.io/redos-checker
An online check of regular expressions for potential ReDoS vulnerabilities.
